question: How do I set up EtherCAT communication on a FlexPro drive?
answer_summary: The FlexPro EtherCAT communication manual covers the protocol setup including ESI file installation, state machine transitions, and object dictionary configuration for FE-series drives. Key addresses: 1000, 6010. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What is the difference between SDO and PDO messages in EtherCAT?
section: Communication Manual / SDO vs. PDO Messages
answer_summary: SDO (Service Data Object) messages are used for non-cyclic parameter read/write access, while PDO (Process Data Object) messages provide real-time cyclic data exchange for control and monitoring. Key addresses: 2065, 60C2, 1A00, 1600. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I configure PDO mapping on a FlexPro EtherCAT drive?
section: Communication Manual / SDO vs. PDO Messages
answer_summary: RPDOs are used by the host to write data to drive objects, and TPDOs are used to read data. PDO mapping is configured through objects 1600h-1603h (RPDO) and 1A00h-1A03h (TPDO). Key addresses: 6040, 1600, 2001, 1A00. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What are the default TxPDO and RxPDO assignments for FlexPro EtherCAT?
section: Communication Manual / SDO vs. PDO Messages
answer_summary: Default TxPDOs include Status Word, Actual Position, Actual Velocity, and Actual Torque. Default RxPDOs include Control Word, Target Position, Target Velocity, and Target Torque. Key addresses: 6040, 1600, 2001, 1A00. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What is the EtherCAT state machine and how does it work?
answer_summary: The EtherCAT state machine has four states: Init, Pre-Operational, Safe-Operational, and Operational. Transitions between states are controlled by the EtherCAT master. Key addresses: 1000h, 0x0000, 0x0FFF. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I configure the Sync Manager watchdog on an EtherCAT drive?
answer_summary: The Sync Manager watchdog monitors PDO communication and the PDO watchdog monitors cyclic data timing. If communication is lost, the drive enters a safe state based on the watchdog configuration. Key addresses: 2065, 60C2. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What ESI file do I need for a FlexPro EtherCAT drive?
answer_summary: The ESI (EtherCAT Slave Information) XML file is named AMC_FP_xx.xx.xx.xx.xml. Always make a backup copy before updating. The file is needed by the EtherCAT master for device configuration. Key addresses: 1000, 6010. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What homing methods are available on FlexPro EtherCAT drives?
answer_summary: Homing is configured through objects 607Ch (Home Offset), 6099h (Homing Speeds), 609Ah (Homing Acceleration), and 6098h (Homing Method). Multiple methods using limit switches and index pulses are supported. Key addresses: 205B, 6041h. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do homing methods 11 to 14 work with the negative limit switch?
answer_summary: Methods 11 to 14 use a negative limit switch instead of a positive limit switch. The initial direction of travel is reversed (left instead of right) compared to methods using the positive limit switch. Key addresses: 6060h, 2039. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I change the mode of operation on an EtherCAT drive?
answer_summary: Use object 6060h (Modes Of Operation) to send a mode change request and read object 6061h (Modes Of Operation Display) to confirm the active mode. Supported modes include Profile Position, Profile Velocity, Cyclic Synchronous, and Homing. Key addresses: 6086h, 6061h, 6060h, 203Ch. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I set up CANopen communication on a FlexPro drive?
answer_summary: The FlexPro CANopen comm manual covers NMT state machine, SDO/PDO messaging, heartbeat monitoring, and the full object dictionary for FlexPro CANopen (FD-series) drives. Key addresses: 100Ch, 2315h. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_CANopen.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What should the CAN bus load be kept below for reliable operation?
answer_summary: It is best to keep the network idle for at least 50% of the time (50% bus load). Bus load depends on the number of nodes, baud rate, and PDO cycle time. Key addresses: 100Ch, 2315h. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_CANopen.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I configure PDO mapping on a CANopen drive?
section: Communication Manual / SDO vs. PDO Messages
answer_summary: PDO configuration is accomplished by setting the communication parameter (COB-ID, transmission type) and mapping parameter (object indices and sub-indices mapped to the PDO). Key addresses: 208D, 1400h, 20E6. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_CANopen.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What NMT states does a CANopen drive support?
answer_summary: The drive supports Bootup, Stopped, Operational, and Pre-operational NMT states. State 0 is Bootup, state 4 is Stopped, state 5 is Operational, and state 127 is Pre-operational. Key addresses: 5535, 1016h, 1017h. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_CANopen.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What CAN hardware do I need to communicate with AMC drives?
answer_summary: A CAN interface card or USB-to-CAN adapter is required. The manual covers CAN card setup and API access for communicating with the CANopen object dictionary. Key addresses: 2100h, 1000h, 2200h, 100Ch. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_CANopen.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I set the CANopen node ID and baud rate in ACE?
answer_summary: In the Network configuration page, set the CANopen Address (Node ID, range 1-127) and Baud Rate (CAN bus speed in kbit/s). Interface Access Control can also be configured. Key addresses: 6060. For the full guide, download "AMC_SW_Manual_ACE.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure the CANopen node ID and baud rate in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In the Network settings, set the CANopen Address (Node ID, 1-127) and Baud Rate. Interface Access Control settings are also available for read-only vs. read-write access. Key addresses: 6060. For the full guide, download "AMC_SW_Manual_DriveWare.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I set up Modbus RTU communication with a DigiFlex drive?
section: Modbus Communication Protocol / Modbus Command Format
answer_summary: Modbus uses function codes for reading/writing coils and registers. The protocol uses an address byte, function code, data bytes, and CRC for message framing. Key addresses: 0308, 0286, 0288, 0268. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_Modbus.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What Modbus function codes are supported by AMC drives?
section: Modbus Communication Protocol / Modbus Command Format
answer_summary: Supported function codes include 01 (Read Coil Status), 02 (Read Input Status), 03 (Read Holding Registers), 05 (Force Single Coil), and 15 (Force Multiple Coils). Key addresses: FF00, 0000. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_Modbus.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I initiate an index or sequence over Modbus?
answer_summary: Register 4003 (hex FA2) is used to initiate Index/Sequence commands. Write the index or sequence number to trigger the desired motion profile. Key addresses: 4001, 4024, 4000. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_Modbus.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I set up Ethernet/IP communication on a FlexPro drive?
section: Serial Communication Protocol / Modes of Operation
answer_summary: FlexPro Ethernet/IP drives support Profile Position, Profile Velocity, Cyclic Synchronous, Homing, and Current modes. Implicit I/O messaging provides real-time cyclic data exchange. Key addresses: 0011, 0001, 0000. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_EthernetIP_FP.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How does implicit I/O messaging work on Ethernet/IP drives?
answer_summary: Implicit I/O messaging requires no further configuration beyond setting up the connection. Actual Position is available as a DINT tag for real-time monitoring. Key addresses: 5000, FC1B, 1769. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_EthernetIP_FP.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I set the IP address on a FlexPro Ethernet/IP drive?
answer_summary: The IP address is configured through the command dictionary. Example format: C0 A8 64 64 corresponds to 192.168.100.100. Key addresses: 0011, 0001, 0000. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_EthernetIP_FP.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I set up POWERLINK communication?
answer_summary: POWERLINK is based on the ISO OSI model. Communication uses isochronous and asynchronous phases with PReq/PRes polling for deterministic real-time data exchange. Key addresses: 2000h, 1000h, 1FFFh, 0x88AB. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_POWERLINK.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How does POWERLINK node addressing work?
answer_summary: POWERLINK uses a managed node (MN) and controlled node (CN) architecture. Each CN has a unique node ID. The cycle includes SoC, PReq/PRes exchange, and SoA frames. Key addresses: 2000h, 1000h, 1FFFh, 0x88AB. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_POWERLINK.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What modes of operation do POWERLINK drives support?
answer_summary: POWERLINK drives support Profile Position, Profile Velocity, Cyclic Synchronous Position, Cyclic Synchronous Velocity, Cyclic Synchronous Torque, and Homing modes. Key addresses: 205B, 6041h. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_POWERLINK.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I set up RS-485 serial communication with a DigiFlex drive?
answer_summary: The RS-485 serial protocol supports multi-drop communication. Configuration includes setting the baud rate, drive address, and proper termination of the bus. Key addresses: D02Eh, 2007, 2008. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_RS485.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What is the serial protocol message format for RS-485 drives?
answer_summary: The FlexPro serial protocol is a byte-based command/response protocol. Messages include start byte (A5h), address, command, data section, and CRC for error checking. Key addresses: 200Fh, 2021h, 2023h, 2024h. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_FP_Serial.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I wire the RS-232 connection for CAN drives?
section: AN-004 / RS232 Connection for CAN Drives
answer_summary: For CAN-based DigiFlex drives, RS-232 is used for commissioning via DriveWare. Set Interface Access Control appropriately and connect using a standard DB-9 serial cable. Key addresses: 3012, 1338, 3805, 1935. For the full guide, download "Application Note 004" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What is the CAN bus cabling guide for CANopen networks?
answer_summary: Use twisted-pair cabling with proper termination (120 ohm resistors) at each end of the bus. RJ-45 connectors can be used for PCB-mount DigiFlex (DZC) drives. Key addresses: 3012, 1000, 3805, 1935. For the full guide, download "Application Note 005" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I wire the CAN bus with proper termination?
answer_summary: All drives must use the same bit rate but each drive must have a unique node ID. Terminate both ends of the CAN bus with 120-ohm resistors between CAN_H and CAN_L. Key addresses: 3012, 1000, 3805, 1935. For the full guide, download "Application Note 005" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What are the FlexPro PCB mount drive terminal pin assignments?
section: Products and System Requirements / Terminal Pins
answer_summary: Pin assignments include T1 (STO-1 INPUT), T2 (STO RETURN), T3 (STO-2 INPUT), T4 (LOGIC PWR), T5 (HV), and motor/feedback connections on remaining pins. Key addresses: E100, E060. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_FlexPro_PCB.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What communication protocols are available on FlexPro drives?
section: Products and System Requirements / Communication Protocols
answer_summary: FlexPro drives support EtherCAT (FE models), CANopen (FD models), Ethernet/IP (FMP-IPM models), RS485/232 (FM-RM and FD-RM models), and POWERLINK depending on model variant. Key addresses: 1000, A402, 6010. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_FlexPro_PCB.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I wire a FlexPro PCB mount drive for DC power?
section: Integration in the Servo System / Wiring
answer_summary: Connect shielded DC power, motor power, and signal wires following the recommended interface PCB layout. Ensure proper chassis earth grounding and use isolated DC supply where required. Key addresses: 1250, 6200. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_FlexPro_PCB.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the PCB design guidelines for FlexPro embedded drives?
section: Integration in the Servo System / PCB Design
answer_summary: Before designing a PCB interface for FlexPro drives, review the recommended reference designs. Follow proper trace routing, clearance, and grounding practices for reliable operation. Key addresses: FACE, 2021, 0224, 610D. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_FlexPro_PCB.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I connect the USB interface to a FlexPro drive?
section: Integration in the Servo System / USB Interface
answer_summary: The USB interface is used for commissioning via ACE software. Follow the recommended USB interface circuit design shown in the hardware manual. Key addresses: 1250, 6200. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_FlexPro_PCB.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the connector types for DigiFlex Performance panel mount drives?
section: Integration in the Servo System / Connectors
answer_summary: DPE drives use 6-pin 3.96mm friction lock headers for signals and 4-port 7.62mm connectors for DC power and motor power connections. Specific crimp tools are listed per connector. Key addresses: 0522, 0800. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_DigiFlex_Panel_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What is the feedback connector pinout for DigiFlex panel mount drives?
section: Integration in the Servo System / Feedback Connector
answer_summary: The 26-pin FEEDBACK connector (J3) on DPE panel drives supports incremental encoder (A+/A-/B+/B-/Z+/Z-), absolute encoder (EnDat 2.2 or BiSS-C via DATA+/DATA-/CLK+/CLK-), Hall sensors (HALL A/B/C), and tachometer (+/-10V analog) inputs — the active pins depend on the feedback type configured in software. Use shielded twisted-pair cable for all encoder and Hall signals, and connect cable shield to the connector shell for noise immunity. For the full guide, download the DigiFlex Performance Panel EtherCAT Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I wire the DC power and motor connections on a DigiFlex panel drive?
section: Integration in the Servo System / Power Connector
answer_summary: The DC POWER / MOTOR POWER connector on DPE panel drives uses a 7.62mm pitch enclosed friction-lock header: pin 1 is HV+ (high-voltage DC positive), pin 2 is HV- (high-voltage DC negative / DC return), and pins 3–5 carry motor phase outputs (Phase A, B, C for brushless or Phase A/B for brushed). A separate PE (protective earth) connection is required on the chassis ground stud. Use wire gauge appropriate for the drive's peak current rating; for example, 030A400 drives require a minimum of 14 AWG for power connections. For the full guide, download the DigiFlex Performance Panel EtherCAT Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I mount a DigiFlex PCB drive with screws?
section: Integration in the Servo System / Mounting
answer_summary: DZ PCB mount drives can be mounted directly on a carrier board using the 2mm pitch board-to-board connector, or secured with M2.5 screws through the designated mounting holes for added mechanical stability in high-vibration applications. The mounting holes accept M2.5 hardware; observe the keep-out areas around the power components and connector zones as shown in the mechanical drawing. AMC's MC1XDZ interface carrier board is also available and provides screw-terminal connectors, encoder interface, CAN transceiver, and RS-232/485 circuitry in a single assembly. For the full guide, download the DigiFlex Performance PCB Mount CANopen Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the CAN transceiver wiring recommendations for DZ drives?
section: Integration in the Servo System / Communication Wires
answer_summary: DZ PCB drives expose a CAN differential pair (CAN_H and CAN_L) that requires an external 3.3V or 5V CAN transceiver (such as the Texas Instruments SN65HVD230 or compatible) on the carrier board; the recommended transceiver circuit is shown in the hardware manual and application note AN-008. Use 120-ohm termination resistors at both ends of the CAN bus, connect the bus using twisted-pair cable (100–120 ohm characteristic impedance), and keep stub lengths under 0.3m. All nodes must share the same baud rate, and each DZ drive must have a unique node ID configured via DIP switches or software. For the full guide, download the DigiFlex Performance PCB Mount CANopen Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the AxCent PCB mount drive specifications?
section: Products and System Requirements / Analog PWM Servo Drive Basics
answer_summary: AxCent PCB mount drives (AZB series) are single- or three-axis analog PWM servo drives with power ratings from 6A/8V (AZB6A8) to 60A/8V (AZB60A8) continuous, using pulse-width modulation at a fixed switching frequency for current control — the PWM on-time is proportional to commanded current. They accept a +/-10V analog command input (100k ohm input impedance), operate from DC supply voltages up to 90V, and mount on the optional MC1XAZ01-HR carrier board which provides screw terminals and interface circuits. AZB models support brushed or brushless motors; the AZBD variant adds a deadband adjustment for brushed voice-coil applications. For the full guide, download the AxCent PCB Mount Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What feedback options does the AxCent drive support?
answer_summary: AxCent PCB drives (AZB/AZBD) support Hall sensors (A/B/C, 5V supply from drive) for brushless motor commutation, a +/-10V analog tachometer input for velocity feedback in closed-loop velocity mode, and a 5V single-ended incremental encoder (A/B/Z channels) for position or velocity feedback — the encoder input can also be used in combination with Halls. The AZBH variant is optimized for Hall-only velocity control, while the AZBE variant supports encoder-based position control. All models include a +/-10V command input and a separate Enable input (active high, 3.3–5V compatible). For the full guide, download the AxCent PCB Mount Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What operating modes are available on AxCent drives?
section: Products and System Requirements / Modes of Operation
answer_summary: AxCent AZB drives support five control modes selectable via DIP switch or solder jumper: Current (Torque) mode — inner current loop only, command scales to peak current; Duty Cycle (Open Loop) — command directly sets PWM duty cycle with no feedback; Hall Velocity — closed-loop velocity using Hall sensor transitions for speed measurement (AZB/AZBH); Tachometer Velocity — closed-loop velocity using a +/-10V tachometer signal; and Analog Position — closed-loop position using incremental encoder feedback (AZBE). The AZBD variant adds a deadband feature useful for voice-coil actuators. DIP switch SW1 selects mode and phase configuration; refer to the hardware manual table for the specific bit pattern. For the full guide, download the AxCent PCB Mount Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I wire power and motor connections for an analog servo drive?
section: Integration in the Servo System / Wiring
answer_summary: Install a European-approved over-current protective device (circuit breaker or fuse) rated for the AC input line current in series with the AC supply before the drive or power supply. Wire sizing must comply with LVD requirements: use minimum 14 AWG for 30A-rated drives (B30A40) and 10 AWG for 100A models (B100A400AC). Keep DC power wires (HV+ and HV-) as short as possible; if they exceed 5 feet, add a 1000µF bypass capacitor near the drive's power input terminals. Use shielded cable for motor connections, with the shield connected to earth ground at the drive end only. For the full guide, download the Classic Analog Drives Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the minimum inductance requirements for analog drives?
answer_summary: PWM servo drives produce a high-frequency pulsed voltage output (typically 20–100 kHz switching frequency) that requires a minimum phase-to-phase motor inductance to limit ripple current. For brushless motors, the minimum inductance is typically 500µH phase-to-phase for drives switching at 20 kHz; lower switching frequencies require higher minimum inductance proportionally. If the motor's inductance is too low, add external series inductors (line reactors) in series with each motor phase lead — size them to bring the total phase inductance to the minimum required value. Failure to meet minimum inductance can cause excessive current ripple, overheating, and potential drive damage. For the full guide, download the Classic Analog Drives Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What command input options are available on analog drives?
section: Products and System Requirements / Command Inputs
answer_summary: Classic analog drives (B series) accept a differential +/-10V analog command signal on the CMD+ and CMD- input pins (100k ohm input impedance); this signal commands current, velocity, or position depending on the selected control mode. Full-scale command scaling (e.g., 10V = peak current, or 10V = max velocity) is set via the GAIN potentiometer or DIP switch current-select bits. An input OFFSET potentiometer trims any DC offset on the command signal. Some models also accept a step-and-direction or PWM command input — check the specific drive datasheet for availability. The Enable (inhibit) input must be pulled to logic high (5V) to allow drive output; leaving it unconnected will prevent the drive from operating. For the full guide, download the Classic Analog Drives Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I connect and set up ACE software for a FlexPro drive?
answer_summary: In ACE, open the Connect To Drive window (toolbar or File menu), select the communication channel — USB (recommended for initial setup), RS232, RS485, or Ethernet — and set the drive address (default is 63 for RS232/RS485/USB; for Ethernet, enter the drive's IP address). After clicking Connect, the drive's workspace opens and the current drive configuration is uploaded automatically. Ensure the drive's Interface Access Control is set to allow full read/write access from the selected channel; if another channel has exclusive access, ACE will connect in read-only mode. Install the USB driver from the ACE installation package if connecting via USB for the first time. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure motor parameters in ACE?
answer_summary: In ACE, navigate to Configuring the Drive > Motor and select the motor type: Brushed DC, Brushless (BLDC/PMSM), Stepper, AC Induction, Voice Coil, or Linear Brushless. For brushless motors, set the number of pole pairs (motor poles ÷ 2), the encoder lines-per-revolution (before quadrature multiplication, e.g., 1000 for a 4000-count/rev encoder), and the motor's continuous and peak current limits in Amps. The motor rated speed (RPM) and back-EMF constant (Ke, V/KRPM) are required for AC induction and stepper configurations. Save parameters to EEPROM using the Save to Drive button after completing all motor settings. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I set up commutation sensors (Halls) in ACE?
answer_summary: In ACE under Configuring the Drive > Commutation, enable Hall sensors and select the Hall phasing: 60-degree or 120-degree spacing depending on the motor specification (120-degree is most common for BLDC motors; use 60-degree for motors that specify it). The Hall State Sequence display shows the live Hall A/B/C states as the motor rotates — verify the sequence advances through all six valid states without invalid transitions (all-zeros or all-ones indicate a wiring fault). If the motor runs roughly or in the wrong direction, swap any two motor phase wires and re-run AutoCommutation to re-detect the commutation offset angle. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure digital inputs and outputs in ACE?
answer_summary: In ACE under Configuring the Drive > Inputs/Outputs, each programmable digital input (PDI1–PDI7 on FlexPro) can be assigned a function from a drop-down list: Motion Enable, Positive/Negative Limit Switch, Home Switch, Index Trigger, Capture Input, Fault Reset, Gain Set Select, or user-defined. Each input can be configured as active-high or active-low. Digital outputs (PDO1–PDO4) can be assigned to indicate In-Position, Motion Active, Drive Fault, At Speed, or custom conditions. The I/O Diagnostics tab shows the real-time state of all inputs and outputs as green (active) or grey (inactive) indicators — use this to verify wiring before running motion. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure the external shunt resistor in ACE?
answer_summary: In ACE under Configuring the Drive > Limits > Shunt, enable the external shunt and set the Shunt Resistance value (ohms) and Shunt Power rating (watts) to match the external resistor installed on the drive's SHUNT output terminals. When DC bus voltage exceeds the shunt threshold (typically 75V for 400V-class drives), the drive activates the shunt output for an initial 10ms pulse to bleed excess regenerated energy; the duty cycle then scales with bus voltage. Minimum shunt resistance values by power module: 030A400 = 20 ohm min, 060A400 = 10 ohm min, 100A400 = 10 ohm min. Always verify the shunt resistor's wattage can handle peak regenerative energy; under-rated resistors can overheat and fail. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I run the current loop auto-tune in ACE?
section: Tuning and Commutation / Current Loop Auto-Tuning
answer_summary: In ACE under Tuning and Commutation > Current Loop, click Auto-Tune to launch the current loop auto-tuning wizard; the drive must be enabled and the motor connected with proper feedback configured. The auto-tune injects a test current signal, measures the motor's electrical time constant (L/R), and calculates optimal proportional (Kp) and integral (Ki) current loop gains. The result is displayed as Current Loop Bandwidth in Hz — a typical target is 1–3 kHz for most servo applications. After auto-tune completes, verify the step response on the oscilloscope (Channel 2 set to Current Measured) shows fast rise with minimal overshoot before saving parameters. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I manually tune the current loop in ACE?
section: Tuning and Commutation / Current Loop Tuning
answer_summary: For manual current loop tuning in ACE, open Tuning and Commutation > Current Loop and set the oscilloscope Channel 1 to Current Target and Channel 2 to Current Measured. Start with the integral gain (Ki) at zero and increase proportional gain (Kp) until the measured current rises quickly and tracks the target step without sustained oscillation, then back off 20%. Next, increase Ki gradually until steady-state current error is eliminated without introducing overshoot; typical starting values are Kp = 100–300 and Ki = 10–50 (in drive-specific units — see the manual for scaling). Disable the drive before changing gain values if the motor exhibits unstable behavior, and use small increments when tuning. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I run AutoCommutation in ACE?
answer_summary: Before running AutoCommutation in ACE, verify the motor type, pole pairs, and encoder resolution are correctly set in Motor > Parameters, and ensure the current loop is tuned (auto or manual). The routine runs a Phase Detect step (injects DC current to align the rotor to a known position) followed by a slow rotation to measure the commutation offset angle and encoder/Hall polarity. The motor must be free to rotate 2–3 mechanical turns; remove any hard mechanical stops. If AutoCommutation succeeds, the Commutation Offset angle (in degrees electrical) and feedback polarity flags are stored automatically — verify operation with a short jog before finalizing. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What should I do if Phase Detect fails during AutoCommutation?
answer_summary: If Phase Detect fails in ACE, first check that the continuous current limit is set to at least 25% of peak current — a limit that is too low prevents the routine from generating sufficient torque to align the rotor. Also verify the motor wiring: all three phase wires must be connected (for brushless) and there must be no mechanical brake or hard stop preventing rotor movement. If the routine fails after two automatic retries, try manually increasing the Phase Detect Current (in Tuning > Commutation settings) by 25% increments. For encoder-only motors without Halls, confirm the encoder Z-pulse (index) is present and correctly wired, as Phase Detect relies on finding the index. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I tune the position loop in ACE?
section: Tuning and Commutation / Position Loop Tuning
answer_summary: In ACE, tune the position loop after the current and velocity loops are stable. Use the Position Loop Auto-Tuning wizard or tune manually under Tuning and Commutation > Position Loop: set the oscilloscope Channel 1 to Position Target and Channel 2 to Position Measured, then command a profiled move. Increase proportional gain (Kp) until following error during motion is minimized, then add integral gain (Ki) to eliminate steady-state position error at rest. Add derivative gain (Kd) or velocity feedforward only if needed to reduce overshoot. The In-Position window and In-Position Time parameters (under Motion Tasks) define when the drive asserts the In-Position output — set these to appropriate values for your application tolerance. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure homing parameters in ACE?
answer_summary: In ACE under Motion Tasks > Homing, configure: Fast Velocity (speed in RPM or user units for the initial switch-search phase), Slow Velocity (reduced speed for index pulse or home-switch-edge detection), Acceleration and Deceleration for both phases, and the Home Offset (units in encoder counts applied after the home position is found). Select the Homing Method that matches your hardware: common options include Homing on negative limit switch with index pulse (Method 1), positive limit switch with index (Method 2), or home switch only without index (Method 17/18). The homing sequence is initiated from the Motion Panel, a digital input trigger, or a network command (object 6098h). For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I set up and run motion indexes in ACE?
answer_summary: In ACE under Motion Tasks > Motion Tasks, create Index entries by specifying: Index Type (Absolute or Relative), Target Position (in user units or encoder counts), Maximum Velocity, Acceleration, Deceleration, and a Dwell time after the move completes. Up to 128 indexes can be stored in the drive's motion engine. Trigger an index from the Motion Panel (for testing), assign a digital input to trigger a specific index number on a rising edge, or initiate via network command (CANopen: write to object 607Ah and set bit 4 of the controlword). Link indexes into sequences using Sequence Tasks to create automated multi-step motion programs. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I use PVT (Position-Velocity-Time) motion profiles in ACE?
answer_summary: In ACE under Motion Tasks > PVT, build a PVT table by defining rows of (Time interval in ms, Velocity at endpoint in RPM or user units, Delta Position in user units). The drive uses cubic spline interpolation between points to produce smooth jerk-limited motion. A PVT buffer holds up to 255 points; ensure the host streams new points before the buffer empties using the buffer-level output object. I/O sync can be configured to assert a digital output at a specific PVT table step — useful for triggering external devices at precise trajectory points. PVT mode is initiated by setting the drive's Mode of Operation to PVT (mode 7), then sending Start PVT via the controlword. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I use the digital oscilloscope in ACE?
answer_summary: The ACE digital oscilloscope (Tools > Digital Oscilloscope) captures up to 4 simultaneous channels of drive-internal signals at the servo loop sample rate (typically 4–16 kHz). Assign signals to each channel from the drop-down: common combinations are Channel 1 = Position Target + Channel 2 = Position Measured (to check following error), or Channel 1 = Current Target + Channel 2 = Current Measured (for current loop tuning). Set the Trigger source, level, and pre/post-trigger window, then click Arm — the scope captures automatically when the trigger condition is met. Use Time/Div and the zoom controls to inspect transients; export data as CSV via File > Export for external analysis. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I use the Motion Engine in ACE?
answer_summary: The Motion Engine in ACE is the drive's onboard task scheduler for executing Indexes, Sequences, and Homing autonomously. States: Disabled (no motion possible), Ready (waiting for a trigger), Running (executing a task), and Error (fault during motion). Enable the Motion Engine by clicking the Enable button in the toolbar (or writing to the Motion Engine enable object over the network) — the drive must already be in Operation Enabled state. Trigger tasks by number from the Motion Panel for testing, via a digital input edge (assign Trigger Index N to a PDI), or via a network command. The Motion Engine can also be configured to auto-start a sequence on power-up via the Start-Up Task setting. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I download firmware to a FlexPro drive using ACE?
answer_summary: In ACE, go to File > Download Firmware (or the Firmware button in the toolbar). Use Browse Folder to point to the directory containing the firmware .bin file, or Browse File to select a specific file; firmware files are named by drive family and version (e.g., FlexPro_v2.xx.xx.bin). ACE downloads the firmware over the active communication channel — USB or RS485 recommended (Ethernet is supported but slower). Do NOT disconnect the drive or cut power during the download; if the process is interrupted, use the recovery mode documented in the manual to restore the drive. After download, ACE will prompt you to power-cycle the drive to activate the new firmware. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I connect DriveWare to a DigiFlex Performance drive?
answer_summary: Launch DriveWare and click New Connection; select RS-232 (for DP panel drives via DB-9 serial cable), RS-485/CANopen, or USB depending on the drive's available interface. For RS-232, set baud rate to 9600 (default) and COM port to match your PC's serial port; the default drive address is 63. After connecting, the Bridge Status indicator in the lower toolbar shows the drive state (red = fault, yellow = not enabled, green = enabled). Use the Waveform Generator (Tools menu) for open-loop testing and the Motion Panel to jog or trigger indexes. If the connection fails, verify the Interface Access Control setting in the drive is not locked to a different channel. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I view drive hardware information in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare, go to Help > Drive Information to open the Drive Information panel, which displays: firmware version (e.g., 5.x.x.x), control board revision, power module part number and current/voltage rating, drive serial number, and MAC address (for Ethernet-capable drives). This panel is useful when reporting issues to AMC support. Also accessible via the status bar at the bottom of the DriveWare window, which shows current drive state, active faults, and bus voltage in real time. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure motor parameters in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare, navigate to Configuring the Drive > Motor and select Motor Type: Brushed DC, Brushless (BLDC), Stepper, or AC Induction. For brushless motors set the number of pole pairs (motor poles ÷ 2) and encoder lines-per-revolution. Set Continuous Current Limit (max sustained output in Amps) and Peak Current Limit (max short-term current); the peak-to-continuous ratio determines the foldback time constant. For steppers, also set the number of steps per revolution and microstepping resolution. Click Save to EEPROM (or use File > Save Parameters) after completing configuration. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure velocity feedback in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare under Configuring the Drive > Velocity Feedback, select the velocity source: Motor Encoder Derived (calculates velocity from encoder count differentiation — most accurate for high-resolution encoders), Hall Velocity (derives velocity from Hall transition timing — useful when no encoder is present), or Tachometer (+/-10V analog tachometer input). Set the Velocity Feedback Filter cutoff frequency to reduce noise; a typical starting value is 500–2000 Hz. For encoder-derived velocity, also set the Velocity Feedback Resolution (post-quadrature encoder counts used for velocity calculation). Hall-derived velocity is less accurate at low speeds; switch to encoder-derived when encoders are available. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I run AutoCommutation in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare, navigate to Configuring the Drive > Commutation and click AutoCommutation. The routine performs Phase Detect (locks rotor with DC current) then slowly rotates the motor to measure the commutation angle offset and verify encoder/Hall polarity. Prerequisites: motor parameters (pole pairs, encoder resolution) must be set, current loop must be tuned, and the motor must rotate freely for 2–3 turns without mechanical stops. If the routine succeeds, it writes the Commutation Offset angle and Encoder Polarity flags to EEPROM automatically. Verify with a jog move: smooth, controllable rotation at commanded velocity indicates correct commutation; erratic motion or excessive current suggests a phase wiring issue. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure digital input functions in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare under Configuring the Drive > Digital I/O, each digital input (DI1–DI8 depending on drive model) has a Function drop-down: Motion Enable (must be asserted to enable the drive bridge), Set Position (latches current position to a preset value on rising edge), Positive/Negative Limit Switch, Home Switch, Index Trigger, Fault Reset, or Capture. Set Polarity (active-high or active-low) for each input to match hardware wiring. Digital outputs (DO1–DO4) can indicate: In-Position, Motion Active, At Speed, Fault, or custom program-controlled states. The I/O Status display shows live input/output states for wiring verification. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What are the minimum shunt resistance values for DigiFlex drives?
answer_summary: Minimum external shunt resistance values by DigiFlex power module (to prevent overcurrent damage to the shunt FET): 015A400 = no external shunt available/N/A; 030A400 = 20 ohm minimum; 060A400 = 10 ohm minimum; 100A400 = 10 ohm minimum. For 800V-class power modules, consult the factory for shunt requirements. In DriveWare under Configuring the Drive > Shunt, enter the actual shunt resistance and rated wattage; the drive uses these values to compute the shunt duty-cycle limit to prevent overheating. The shunt activates when DC bus voltage exceeds the configured shunt threshold (nominally 75V for 400V-class drives). For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I tune the current loop in DriveWare?
section: Tuning and Commutation / Current Loop Tuning
answer_summary: In DriveWare under Tuning > Current Loop, follow the 4-step procedure: Step 1 — set Kp (proportional gain) starting from a low value (e.g., 50); Step 2 — enable the drive and use the Waveform Generator to command a current step, observe on the oscilloscope (Channel 1 = Current Target, Channel 2 = Current Measured); Step 3 — increase Kp until current rise is fast with minimal ringing, then back off 10–20%; Step 4 — add Ki (integral gain) to eliminate steady-state error without causing oscillation. For AC Induction motors, also complete Step 7: tune the Flux Reference loop (Kp-flux, Ki-flux) using the magnetizing current response. Save parameters after tuning. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I tune the velocity loop in DriveWare?
section: Tuning and Commutation / Velocity Loop Tuning
answer_summary: In DriveWare under Tuning > Velocity Loop, set the oscilloscope to Channel 1 = Velocity Target and Channel 2 = Velocity Measured. Command a velocity step from the Motion Panel and observe the response. Increase Kp (proportional gain) until the velocity follows the target quickly with minimal overshoot (aim for <10% overshoot), then add Ki (integral gain) in small increments to eliminate steady-state velocity error at constant speed. Add Kd (derivative gain) or Acceleration Feedforward only if needed to reduce velocity lag during ramps. A velocity loop bandwidth of 50–200 Hz is typical for most servo systems. Ensure the current loop bandwidth is at least 5–10x the velocity loop bandwidth. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I run motor commutation in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare, before running the commutation routine go to Events > Advanced and set the Motor Over Speed event Action to Stop Smoothly or Disable (not Fault) to prevent a hard fault during the slow-rotate commutation test. Under Tuning > Commutation, confirm the encoder polarity (should show the encoder count increasing in the positive-command direction) and verify no encoder errors are reported. Click AutoCommutation to launch: the routine injects DC current, aligns the rotor, then rotates it slowly to measure the electrical commutation offset angle. After completion, the Commutation Offset value (degrees electrical) is displayed and saved to EEPROM. Test with a jog command; consistent smooth rotation confirms correct commutation. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I set up homing in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare under Motion Tasks > Homing, configure the following: Home Switch polarity (Active High or Active Low), Limit Switch polarity, Homing Method (combinations of index pulse and switch edges — e.g., Method 1 uses negative limit switch with index pulse; Method 17 uses home switch edge only without index), Fast Velocity (switch-search speed), Slow Velocity (index/edge detection speed), and Home Offset (position value assigned to the home position). The homing sequence begins when initiated from the Motion Panel or via a digital input assigned to Start Homing. After a successful home, the drive's position counter is set to the Home Offset value and the Homed status bit (statusword bit 12) is set. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I create and run Jog profiles in DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare under Motion Tasks > Jog, configure up to 4 Jog Velocity presets (Jog Speed 1–4 in RPM or user units), plus shared Acceleration and Deceleration ramp rates. Jog direction (positive or negative) and speed level are selected from the Motion Panel or via digital input assignments (assign DI functions Jog +Speed1/2 and Jog -Speed1/2 to specific inputs). Pressing and holding the Jog button (or holding the digital input active) runs the motor continuously at the selected speed; releasing the input applies the programmed deceleration ramp to stop. Jog is useful for verifying direction convention and feedback polarity after initial wiring. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I use the digital oscilloscope in DriveWare?
answer_summary: The DriveWare digital oscilloscope (Tools > Oscilloscope) captures up to 4 simultaneous channels of drive-internal signals sampled at the servo loop rate. Add channels by clicking the + button and selecting from the signal list: Velocity Target, Velocity Measured, Current Target, Current Measured, Position Target, Position Error, and others. Configure trigger source, trigger level, and sample window duration, then click Start to arm the capture. After a trigger event, the waveform is displayed and can be zoomed and panned. Use Cursor A/B to measure time intervals and amplitudes. Save captures as .dwc files or export as .csv for analysis in Excel or MATLAB. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I download firmware to a DigiFlex drive using DriveWare?
answer_summary: In DriveWare, go to File > Download Firmware. Use Browse Folder to point to the folder containing the firmware .bin or .fmw file, or Browse File to select a specific firmware file; firmware is named by drive series and version (e.g., DigiFlex_v5.x.x.x.fmw). Download occurs over the active communication channel — RS-232 is standard; CAN is also supported but slower. Do NOT interrupt power or the communication link during the firmware download process; an incomplete download may require factory recovery. After the firmware is downloaded, cycle power to the drive to activate the new firmware, then re-connect with DriveWare and verify the firmware version in Drive Information. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What are the EtherCAT drive control state machine states?
answer_summary: The CiA 402 drive state machine has 8 states controlled via the Controlword (object 6040h) and monitored via the Statusword (object 6041h): Not Ready to Switch On (power-on initialization, not user-accessible), Switch On Disabled (power bridge disabled, drive idle), Ready to Switch On (fault-free, waiting for Switch On command), Switched On (bridge enabled, current loops active, waiting for Enable Operation), Operation Enabled (full servo control active, motor responds to commands), Quick Stop Active (deceleration in progress after Quick Stop command), Fault Reaction Active (fault detected, executing fault reaction), and Fault (fault state, requires Fault Reset command to clear). The standard enable sequence writes Controlword = 6 (Ready to Switch On), then 7 (Switch On), then 15 (Enable Operation). For the full guide, download the FlexPro EtherCAT Communication Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What does the Operation Enabled state mean?
answer_summary: Operation Enabled (Statusword bits 0–5 = 0x37, i.e., bits: Ready=1, Switched On=1, Operation Enabled=1, Fault=0, Voltage Enabled=1, Quick Stop=1) is the normal run state: the power bridge is energized, all control loops (current, velocity, position) are active, and the drive executes commands received via PDO or SDO. To enter this state, the host writes Controlword = 15 (0x000F) while in Switched On state. To leave gracefully, write Controlword = 7 (disable operation), then 6 (switch off) to return to Switch On Disabled. Any fault condition automatically transitions the drive to Fault Reaction Active then Fault; write Controlword bit 7 = 1 (Fault Reset) to return to Switch On Disabled after clearing the fault condition. For the full guide, download the FlexPro EtherCAT Communication Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I troubleshoot a red LED on an analog drive?
answer_summary: Check for over-voltage, under-voltage, and invalid Hall state conditions. For brushless drives, verify the 3 Hall sensor inputs are connected properly and returning valid states. Key addresses: 3012, 5A20, 3805, 1935. For the full guide, download "Application Note 009" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What causes over-voltage shutdown on a servo drive?
answer_summary: If the DC bus voltage exceeds the drive's rated limit, an over-voltage shutdown occurs. Check the AC power line for proper voltage and verify the DC power supply output is within the drive's rated range. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_AnalogDrives.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What causes overload faults on servo drives?
answer_summary: Overload occurs when continuous current exceeds the drive's rating. When using a single phase (brushed) motor with a three phase (brushless) drive, use the 60-degree phase setting. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_AnalogDrives.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What causes jerky or rough motor motion?
answer_summary: Jerky motion is typically caused by improper commutation, usually because the motor phasing or Hall sensor wiring is incorrect. Verify commutation settings and feedback connections. Key addresses: 1023, 7532, 1118, 15A8. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_AnalogDrives.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I troubleshoot excessive heating of the drive and motor?
answer_summary: Excessive heating is characteristic of minimum inductance issues or excessive continuous current draw. Verify minimum inductance requirements are met and reduce current limits if needed. Key addresses: 1023, 7532, 1118, 15A8. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_AnalogDrives.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What fault codes are available in the CANopen object dictionary?
answer_summary: Object 2027.04h provides the Absolute Encoder Fault Word. Emergency (EMCY) messages contain error codes and additional bytes identifying the specific fault condition. Key addresses: 200E, 2027, 2027h. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_CANopen.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What are the emergency error codes for CANopen drives?
answer_summary: Error codes include PVT Sequence Counter Error and various drive fault conditions. The EMCY message contains the error code in bytes 1-2 and additional manufacturer-specific data in bytes 5-8. Key addresses: 1800, 1984, 1A00. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_CANopen.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I read the drive fault status in EtherCAT?
answer_summary: Object 2027.04h contains the Absolute Encoder Fault Word. Additional fault information is available through the Status Word (6041h) and manufacturer-specific fault objects. Key addresses: 200E, 2027, 2027h. For the full guide, download "AMC_CommManual_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I configure Safe Torque Off (STO) on a DigiFlex drive?
answer_summary: STO requires both STO-1 and STO-2 inputs to be active for the drive to operate. The STO operation test must be performed to maintain SIL 3, Cat 4 / PL e certification. Key addresses: 1800, 3849, 1508, 2061. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_DigiFlex_Panel_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I test the STO function on a DigiFlex panel drive?
section: Operation and Features / STO Operation Test
answer_summary: To maintain safety certification, perform the STO operation test by removing the STO input signals and verifying the drive disables motor power within the specified response time. Key addresses: 1800, 3849, 1508, 2061. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_DigiFlex_Panel_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I wire the STO inputs on a FlexPro PCB mount drive?
section: Products and System Requirements / Terminal Pins
answer_summary: STO-1 INPUT is on pin T1, STO RETURN is on pin T2, and STO-2 INPUT is on pin T3. Both STO inputs must be energized for the drive to enable motor power. Key addresses: E100, E060. question: What are the operating temperature ranges for FlexPro drives?

Key addresses: 275D, 461E, 810F, 704F. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_FlexPro_PCB.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the temperature derating curves for DigiFlex panel drives?
section: Products and System Requirements / System Requirements
answer_summary: Maximum ambient temperature and continuous current derating curves are provided per drive model. Higher ambient temperatures require reducing the continuous current limit. Key addresses: A400, B080. For the full guide, download "AMC_HWManual_DigiFlex_Panel_EtherCAT.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I identify an AMC drive part number?
answer_summary: AMC drive part numbers encode the family (FE/FM/FD for FlexPro, DP/DZ/DV for DigiFlex, AZ for AxCent), form factor, current rating, voltage rating, and communication protocol. question: What does the FlexPro part number encoding mean?

question: What does the DigiFlex Performance part number encoding mean?

question: What control modes do FlexPro drives support?

answer_summary: FlexPro drives support Current (Torque), Velocity, Position, Profile Position, Cyclic Synchronous Position/Velocity/Torque, and analog command modes depending on the communication variant. question: What feedback types are supported on FlexPro drives?

question: What feedback types are supported on DigiFlex panel EtherCAT drives?

question: How do I configure encoder feedback on a FlexPro drive?

question: How do I wire an incremental encoder to a DigiFlex panel drive?
answer_summary: Connect encoder A+, A-, B+, B-, Z+, Z- signals to the FEEDBACK connector. Use differential connections for noise immunity. Single-ended encoders require grounding the negative inputs. question: How do I configure Hall sensor feedback?
answer_summary: Hall sensors provide commutation information for brushless motors. Connect Hall A, B, C signals and verify the Hall state sequence matches the motor's electrical angle. question: What is the commutation process for brushless motors?
answer_summary: Motor commutation maintains the correct relationship between stator current and rotor position. Proper commutation requires correct Hall sensor wiring and phase angle configuration. question: How do I use Step and Direction command input on a FlexPro drive?
